channel.title: Drupal Core
channel.description: Stay informed about developments in Drupal Core.

item.title: #3575585: Convert remaining themes to OOP
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal task
  • Version: 11.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: cc89478 · +518/-386 prod · +510/-394 test · 40 files
  • Discussion: #3575585 · 3 contributors · 16 comments

Summary

Drupal themes can now use object-oriented hook implementations instead of procedural functions in .theme files. This update converts all remaining core themes except Claro, Olivero, and the starter kit to the new pattern. Hook implementations now live in dedicated class files with attributes marking which hooks they implement. This modernizes the codebase while maintaining full backward compatibility for existing custom themes.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

Themes previously implemented hooks as procedural functions in .theme files (e.g. themename_preprocess_html()). The new approach places these in a src/Hook/ThemeNameHooks.php class with methods decorated by #[Hook] attributes.

The conversion moved hook implementations from .theme files to classes for test themes (test_base_theme, test_subtheme, test_theme_alter, etc.), the Umami demo theme, and test themes for Views UI. Each theme now has a hooks class in the Drupal\themename\Hook namespace.

For stable9, the .theme file retains the procedural functions marked with #[LegacyHook] attributes, while also creating the OOP hooks class. This demonstrates the backward compatibility layer: themes can keep existing .theme files working while optionally adopting the new pattern. The StringTranslationTrait was added where themes used t() function calls, replacing them with $this->t().

The conversion was largely mechanical, performed using rector. Method names in hook classes use camelCase, and double underscores in hook names (common in preprocess hooks with suggestions) convert to StudlyCase (e.g. preprocess_theme_test_preprocess_suggestions__kitten__flamingo becomes preprocessThemeTestPreprocessSuggestionsKittenFlamingo).

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: February 25, 2026
  • Committed: March 5, 2026 (8 days later)

Key contributors

  • nicxvan (nLightened Development LLC)
  • smustgrave (Mobomo)
  • longwave (Full Fat Things)

Collaboration

The issue opened on Feb 25, 2026 to convert remaining core themes to object-oriented hook implementations. nicxvan created the initial patch, which converted most test themes and the Umami demo theme. longwave requested backward compatibility for stable9, which nicxvan added to demonstrate BC patterns for themes. The patch committed to both main and 11.x branches on March 5, just over a week after opening. This swift timeline reflects the straightforward nature of the conversion after earlier work established the pattern.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3040302: "Edit summary" button for the text with summary widget has unthemable markup
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal feature request
  • Version: 11.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 6f2da24 · +12/-6 prod · +132/-4 test · 12 files
  • Discussion: #3040302 · 14 contributors · 38 comments

Summary

Text fields with summary display have an "Edit summary" or "Hide summary" button that toggles the summary field visibility. Previously, the markup for this button was hardcoded in JavaScript and could not be customized by themes or modules. This made it difficult to adjust the button's appearance or add accessibility features without patching core. The button markup is now generated through the themeable Drupal.theme system, allowing developers to override it using standard JavaScript theming patterns. This provides flexibility for sites with custom design systems or accessibility requirements.

Impact

Affects module maintainers.

  • New API: New Drupal.theme.textEditSummaryButton() JavaScript function allows themes to override the markup of the Edit/Hide summary button on text with summary fields.

Technical details

The text module's JavaScript previously created the Edit/Hide summary button markup using a hardcoded template literal in text.js. This string included the wrapping span, button element, and classes, but was not wrapped in a Drupal.theme() call.

The change extracts this markup into a new Drupal.theme.textEditSummaryButton() function that accepts a title parameter (the translated "Hide summary" or "Edit summary" text). The original JavaScript now calls Drupal.theme('textEditSummaryButton', Drupal.t('Hide summary')) instead of building the markup inline.

The default theme function returns the exact same markup as before, maintaining backward compatibility. Themes or modules can now override this function to customize the button markup, add extra classes, modify the structure, or enhance accessibility.

A test module (text_summary_test) demonstrates the override pattern by adding a custom class and visually-hidden prefix text. The functional JavaScript test verifies that overrides work correctly by checking for the presence of the custom class.

Upgrade

To customize the Edit/Hide summary button markup, override the Drupal.theme.textEditSummaryButton function in your theme or module JavaScript:

Drupal.theme.textEditSummaryButton = function (title) {
  return `<span class="field-edit-link custom-class">
    (<button type="button" class="link link-edit-summary">${title}</button>)
  </span>`;
};

The title parameter contains the translated "Hide summary" or "Edit summary" text. Your override should return an HTML string containing a button element that the text module can attach event handlers to. Ensure your library depends on core/drupal.text so the override loads after the default implementation.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: March 14, 2019
  • Committed: March 5, 2026 (6 years later)

Key contributors

  • rodrigoaguilera
  • sulfikar_s (Zyxware Technologies)
  • ankithashetty (Specbee)
  • smustgrave (Mobomo)
  • mdranove
  • longwave (Full Fat Things)

Collaboration

This issue was opened in March 2019 and received initial patches within days, but progress stalled for several years. Multiple contributors rerolled patches to fix code style issues as Drupal's linting standards evolved. In 2023, a maintainer requested a test and an issue summary update. The key breakthrough came when mdranove created a merge request with a comprehensive test implementation, including a full test module that demonstrated the override pattern. This gave maintainers confidence in the feature's correctness. The change was committed in March 2026, nearly seven years after the initial report, and backported to Drupal 11.x as a new feature.

Lessons learned

  • When JavaScript code generates markup that third-party developers might want to customize, wrap the markup generation in Drupal.theme() functions rather than hard-coding it. This provides a standard extension point that allows themes and modules to override the markup without patching or replacing the entire JavaScript behavior. (via rodrigoaguilera)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3568092: Convert editor.module procedural submit, validate and Ajax callbacks to methods
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal task
  • Version: 11.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 3fb558d · +296/-195 prod · +0/-6 infra · 10 files
  • Discussion: #3568092 · 4 contributors · 22 comments

Summary

The Editor module moved four procedural form callbacks from editor.module into methods within the EditorHooks class. These callbacks handle configuration, validation, submission, and AJAX behavior for the text editor settings form. The procedural functions remain as thin wrappers that trigger deprecation notices and delegate to the new methods. This change aligns with Drupal's broader effort to eliminate procedural code from .module files in favor of object-oriented patterns.

Impact

Affects module maintainers.

  • Deprecation: Four procedural form callbacks in editor.module replaced with methods in EditorHooks class

Technical details

The change relocates four functions that were part of filter_format_form() into the EditorHooks service class: editor_form_filter_admin_format_editor_configure(), editor_form_filter_admin_form_ajax(), editor_form_filter_admin_format_validate(), and editor_form_filter_admin_format_submit(). Each procedural function now triggers a deprecation error and forwards to the corresponding method.

The new methods are: editorFormFilterAdminFormatEditorConfigure(), editorFormFilterAdminFormAjax(), editorFormFilterAdminFormatValidate(), and editorFormFilterAdminFormatSubmit(). All form alter logic in EditorHooks::formFilterFormatFormAlter() was updated to reference these methods using self::class . ':methodName' syntax for callbacks.

References to the old function names in ckeditor5.module and CKEditor5.php were updated with @see tags pointing to the new methods. The conversion also allowed removal of a PHPStan baseline entry for a missing return type. The functions handle editor selection changes, AJAX updates, validation of editor configuration, and saving editor entities when the filter format form is submitted.

Upgrade

The four deprecated procedural functions still work but will be removed in Drupal 12. If your custom code references these functions directly, update to use the EditorHooks service methods instead:

// Before:
editor_form_filter_admin_format_editor_configure($form, $form_state);

// After:
\Drupal::service(\Drupal\editor\Hook\EditorHooks::class)
  ->editorFormFilterAdminFormatEditorConfigure($form, $form_state);

Similarly for editorFormFilterAdminFormAjax(), editorFormFilterAdminFormatValidate(), and editorFormFilterAdminFormatSubmit(). If you reference these functions as form callbacks, use EditorHooks::class . ':methodName' format.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: January 17, 2026
  • Committed: March 5, 2026 (1 month later)

Key contributors

  • claudiu.cristea (Webikon)
  • dcam
  • nicxvan (nLightened Development LLC)
  • longwave (Full Fat Things)

Collaboration

This issue opened in January 2026 as part of the meta-issue to eliminate core .module files. The initial patch had clean automated test results. Review by dcam caught two documentation references in the CKEditor5 module that still pointed to the old function names. Claudiu Cristea quickly updated these with @see tags pointing to the new methods.

After initial approval, longwave identified a conflict when applying to the 11.x branch due to other deprecation work. This required splitting the work into two merge requests: one for 11.x that deprecates the functions, and one for main that removes them entirely. The author created both MRs and both were committed the same day in March after the reroll.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3570849: Deprecate SessionManager::delete()
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal task
  • Version: 11.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 29b81b3 · +186/-68 prod · +114/-0 test · 14 files
  • Discussion: #3570849 · 4 contributors · 19 comments

Summary

Session management now uses a dedicated repository service instead of having SessionManager directly access the database. This makes it easier for modules to replace session storage backends without overriding the entire session manager. The new approach also removes unnecessary checks that prevented deleting sessions in certain contexts, since the repository can delete any user's sessions regardless of the current user context.

Impact

Affects module maintainers.

  • Deprecation: SessionManager::delete() and SessionManager::setWriteSafeHandler() are deprecated; use UserSessionRepositoryInterface::deleteAll() instead.
  • New API: New UserSessionRepositoryInterface service provides deleteAll() method for managing user sessions.

Technical details

The SessionManager::delete() method previously performed direct database queries to remove user sessions. This was the last remaining database operation in SessionManager, making it difficult for modules to swap out session storage backends without extending the entire class.

The responsibility for deleting user sessions now belongs to the new UserSessionRepository service implementing UserSessionRepositoryInterface. The repository's deleteAll() method accepts a user ID and removes all session records for that user.

The old SessionManager::delete() guarded deletions with WriteSafeSessionHandler checks and CLI detection. These guards are removed in the new repository because deleteAll() can target any user's sessions, not just the current session. For example, when blocking a user, Drupal deletes all their sessions regardless of who is currently authenticated.

The SessionManager constructor signature changed: the database connection parameter is removed and the session handler parameter moved to match the parent class order. A new UserSessionRepositoryInterface parameter was added. Backward compatibility detects the old parameter order and triggers a deprecation error.

Core code in User::postSave() now injects and uses UserSessionRepositoryInterface instead of calling SessionManager::delete().

Upgrade

If your code calls SessionManager::delete(), inject UserSessionRepositoryInterface instead and call deleteAll():

// Before:
$session_manager = \Drupal::service('session_manager');
$session_manager->delete($uid);

// After:
$session_repository = \Drupal::service(UserSessionRepositoryInterface::class);
$session_repository->deleteAll($uid);

If you extend SessionManager and override the constructor, update your parameter list to match the new signature with the session handler as the second parameter and UserSessionRepositoryInterface as the last parameter.

The SessionManager::setWriteSafeHandler() method is also deprecated with no replacement needed.

To provide a custom session storage backend, implement UserSessionRepositoryInterface and tag your service with backend_overridable to override the default implementation.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: January 31, 2026
  • Committed: March 5, 2026 (1 month later)

Key contributors

  • znerol
  • dcam
  • godotislate
  • longwave (Full Fat Things)

Collaboration

This issue revisited an idea first attempted 10 years earlier in #2472535, which had a different approach. The author recognized this connection during the current work.

The initial merge request needed a change record URL update. During review, dcam suggested also deprecating the connection property and constructor argument, which led to reorganizing the constructor parameters to match the parent class. This made the change cleaner but required backward compatibility logic to detect the old parameter order.

longwave identified that the change could land in 11.4 and be removed in 12.0, noting that very few contrib modules extend SessionManager. The timeline from opening to commit was just over one month, with a follow-up issue created immediately after commit to track the removal work for Drupal 12.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3576995: Update CKEditor 5 to 47.6.0
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Critical task
  • Version: 11.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: be296a7 · +420/-420 · 400 files
  • Discussion: #3576995 · 2 contributors · 20 comments

Summary

CKEditor 5 has been updated from version 47.5.x to 47.6.0. This update includes bug fixes, performance improvements, and other enhancements from the upstream CKEditor 5 project. The update ensures Drupal's rich text editor stays current with the latest stable release, providing users with improved editing capabilities and continued compatibility with modern browsers.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

This is a routine vendor library update that brings CKEditor 5 to version 47.6.0. The diff shows changes across all CKEditor 5 JavaScript asset files in core/assets/vendor/ckeditor5/. The primary visible changes are copyright year updates from 2025 to 2026 throughout the codebase, indicating this is a maintenance release prepared early in 2026.

The update follows Drupal's standard practice of tracking CKEditor 5 releases closely to provide users with the latest editor features and fixes. All changes are contained within the vendored JavaScript files, with no modifications to Drupal's integration layer. The minified nature of the assets means the detailed changes are not easily readable from the diff, but this is expected for vendor library updates.

The update was committed to multiple branches (11.x, 11.3.x, 10.6.x, and main), ensuring consistency across supported Drupal versions. This approach maintains feature parity and security posture across the release branches that include CKEditor 5.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: March 4, 2026
  • Committed: March 5, 2026

Key contributors

  • longwave (Full Fat Things)
  • quietone (PreviousNext)
  • nod_ (Très Bien Tech)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3576556: Deprecate CachePluginBase::cacheExpire
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal task
  • Version: 11.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: e123a46 · +30/-20 · 6 files
  • Discussion: #3576556 · 3 contributors · 10 comments

Summary

Views cache plugins included an outdated method from Drupal 7 that retroactively checked cache expiration by comparing timestamps. This approach is unnecessary because Drupal's cache system already handles expiration when cache items are stored. The method has been deprecated and is no longer called internally. This simplifies the Views caching logic and removes confusion about how cache expiration works in Views.

Impact

Affects module maintainers.

  • Deprecation: CachePluginBase::cacheExpire() deprecated with no replacement; custom Views cache plugins should remove overrides of this method.

Technical details

The CachePluginBase::cacheExpire() method was a holdover from Drupal 7 caching that attempted to validate cache freshness by comparing $cache->created against a calculated cutoff time during cache retrieval. This was redundant because Drupal's cache backend already enforces expiration based on the expire parameter set during cache->set().

The cacheGet() method previously called cacheExpire() to get a cutoff timestamp, then conditionally restored cached data only if !$cutoff || $cache->created > $cutoff. Since core implementations either returned FALSE (Tag plugin) or calculated a cutoff based on lifespan (Time plugin), this logic duplicated what the cache backend already handles through proper max-age and expiration settings via cacheSetMaxAge().

The method is now deprecated with a trigger error, and the call has been removed from cacheGet(). Cached results are now restored directly when found, relying on the cache backend to return only valid entries. Core implementations in the Time and Tag plugins also add the deprecation trigger while maintaining their return values for backward compatibility.

Upgrade

Custom Views cache plugins that override CachePluginBase::cacheExpire() should remove their implementation. If your plugin needs time-based expiration, implement it by returning the appropriate value from cacheSetMaxAge() instead, which sets the cache expire parameter when storing cache entries.

Before:

protected function cacheExpire($type) {
  return $this->time->getRequestTime() - 3600;
}

After: Remove the method entirely. Use cacheSetMaxAge() to control expiration:

protected function cacheSetMaxAge($type) {
  return 3600;
}

See the change record at #3576855 for details.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: March 2, 2026
  • Committed: March 5, 2026 (2 days later)

Key contributors

  • berdir (MD Systems GmbH)
  • lendude (iO)
  • catch (Third and Grove)
  • sivaji_ganesh_jojodae

Collaboration

This cleanup was identified by berdir while working on the views_custom_cache_tag module. The confusion about the difference between cacheExpire() and cacheSetMaxAge() prompted investigation, revealing this was a remnant from Drupal 7. lendude confirmed by checking the Views D7 repository that this was indeed legacy code. catch committed the straightforward deprecation within three days of the issue being opened.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3575171: Add Navigation to the Standard profile and recipes
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Critical task
  • Version: 11.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 4dc572d · +35/-25 prod · +60/-64 test · 22 files
  • Discussion: #3575171 · 5 contributors · 21 comments

Summary

Drupal's Standard profile and standard recipe now install the Navigation module instead of Toolbar by default. This change makes the modern Navigation interface the default administrative toolbar for new Drupal sites. The content editor role now uses the access navigation permission instead of access toolbar. This transition positions Navigation as the standard administrative experience for Drupal, replacing the legacy Toolbar module.

Impact

Affects site owners.

  • Permission change: The access toolbar permission is replaced by access navigation in the Standard profile's content_editor role.

Technical details

The Standard installation profile (standard.info.yml) now installs the navigation module instead of toolbar. The content_editor role configuration (user.role.content_editor.yml) grants access navigation permission instead of access toolbar, with the module dependency updated accordingly.

The standard recipe (core/recipes/standard/recipe.yml) follows suit, declaring navigation instead of toolbar in its install list. The recipe explicitly imports Navigation's configuration files: navigation.block_layout, navigation.settings, system.menu.content, and system.menu.navigation-user-links.

Performance tests were updated to reflect the different behavior of Navigation compared to Toolbar. Query counts and cache access patterns shifted slightly due to Navigation's implementation. Some queries decreased (37 vs 39 in one test scenario) as Navigation optimizes certain operations differently. The diff shows both forward and reverse patches, indicating the change was applied, then temporarily reverted, then reapplied after fixing breaking tests in the 11.x branch.

Migrate Drupal UI tests required updates to entity counts, as Navigation introduces additional menu entities (10 instead of 8 menus for D6 upgrades, 7 instead of 5 for D7). Demo Umami profile tests also needed adjustments to JavaScript byte counts.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: February 23, 2026
  • First commit: March 3, 2026 (7 days later)
  • Last commit: March 5, 2026 (1 day later)

Key contributors

  • plopesc (Lullabot)
  • catch (Third and Grove)
  • mherchel (Dripyard)
  • longwave (Full Fat Things)
  • quietone (PreviousNext)

Collaboration

This critical task opened on February 23, 2026, and committed within 10 days after four commits. The initial patch successfully replaced Toolbar with Navigation in Standard profile and recipes. However, comment #12 reveals the change broke 11.x pipelines because migrate_drupal_ui tests weren't present in the main branch. The team had to backport test updates to 11.x.

The path involved coordination across multiple test suites: Standard performance tests, Migrate Drupal UI tests for both Drupal 6 and 7 upgrades, and Demo Umami profile tests. Each required precise updates to expected query counts, cache metrics, and entity counts. This cross-cutting change demonstrates the interconnected nature of Drupal's test coverage and the importance of maintaining test parity across branches.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3552827: Replace all expectDeprecation() calls with expectUserDeprecationMessage()
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Major task
  • Version: 11.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 71fceb9 · +364/-364 test · +1/-3 infra · 241 files
  • Discussion: #3552827 · 3 contributors · 20 comments

Summary

Drupal's test suite now uses PHPUnit's native method for expecting deprecation messages. The change replaces all instances of the deprecated expectDeprecation() method with expectUserDeprecationMessage() throughout core tests. This internal cleanup ensures Drupal's testing framework stays aligned with modern PHPUnit standards and allows contributed modules to receive proper deprecation warnings when testing with Drupal 11.4.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

In #3497124, the expectDeprecation() method was deprecated in favor of PHPUnit's native expectUserDeprecationMessage(). This issue completes the transition by replacing all remaining calls across Drupal core's test suite.

The change touches over 100 test files across all subsystems, performing a straightforward method name replacement. No test logic changes were required. The deprecation ignore rule for expectDeprecation() was also removed from core/.deprecation-ignore.txt, allowing Drupal to now report usage of the deprecated method.

The merge requests targeted both the main and 11.x branches to ensure the cleanup reaches Drupal 11.4, where contrib modules testing against that version will start seeing deprecation warnings if they still use expectDeprecation(). This helps the contributed module ecosystem migrate to the new PHPUnit standard before the old method is fully removed in Drupal 12.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: October 17, 2025
  • Committed: March 5, 2026 (4 months later)

Key contributors

  • mondrake
  • godotislate
  • catch (Third and Grove)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3575575: Remove deprecations around File component and module
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal task
  • Version: main
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 3f8e552 · +36/-1373 prod · +0/-372 test · 23 files
  • Discussion: #3575575 · 4 contributors · 23 comments

Summary

Drupal core cleaned up deprecated code related to file handling that was scheduled for removal. This maintenance work removes old code paths and helper functions that modules and sites should have already migrated away from in earlier versions. The change reduces technical debt and simplifies the file handling codebase without affecting external code that has already been updated to use current APIs.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

This issue removed deprecated code in the File component and module that was marked for removal in Drupal 12. The cleanup includes:

Removed deprecated integer constants for file exists behavior (FileSystemInterface::EXISTS_RENAME, EXISTS_REPLACE, EXISTS_ERROR) and the backward compatibility method FileExists::fromLegacyInt(). All file operations now require the FileExists enum.

Removed the LegacyMimeTypeMapLoadedSubscriber event subscriber and the deprecated hook_file_mimetype_mapping_alter(). MIME type customization now requires implementing an event listener for MimeTypeMapLoadedEvent.

Removed deprecated methods from ExtensionMimeTypeGuesser (setMapping(), constructor accepting ModuleHandlerInterface) and MimeTypeMap (getMapping(), setMapping()). Removed legacy mapping arrays from ExtensionMimeTypeGuesser.

Removed deprecated functions from file.module: file_get_content_headers(), file_managed_file_submit(), file_system_settings_submit(), and template preprocess functions now registered directly in hook_theme.

Cleaned up deprecated service properties using DeprecatedServicePropertyTrait from FileSystem and ExtensionMimeTypeGuesser. Removed the Drupal\file\Upload\FileUploadHandler service alias.

Removed all type hints that allowed legacy integer values for FileExists parameters across FileSystemInterface, FileSystem, FileRepository, FileUploadHandler, and migrate's FileCopy process plugin.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: February 25, 2026
  • Committed: March 4, 2026 (7 days later)

Key contributors

  • smustgrave (Mobomo)
  • dcam
  • kim.pepper (PreviousNext)
  • catch (Third and Grove)

Collaboration

This issue moved efficiently from opening to commit in about one week. The initial merge request was comprehensive, removing a large set of related deprecations together. Reviewer dcam provided detailed feedback on remaining legacy type hints and missed deprecations in ExtensionMimeTypeGuesser, which smustgrave quickly addressed. The team confirmed test failures were unrelated random failures before kim.pepper as subsystem maintainer signaled readiness to commit. Catch committed the change, noting the substantial amount of code removed.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3575467: Remove deprecations related to the theme engine
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal task
  • Version: main
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 9f05b58 · +50/-413 prod · +11/-221 test · +0/-6 infra · 35 files
  • Discussion: #3575467 · 4 contributors · 35 comments

Summary

Drupal has removed the legacy theme engine extension system that allowed themes to use different rendering engines. Theme engines are now exclusively implemented as tagged services rather than .engine files. This change completes the conversion started in earlier versions and removes all deprecated code related to scanning, loading, and processing .engine files. The Twig theme engine continues to work as before, but is now registered as a service instead of discovered as a file-based extension.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

Theme engines were previously treated as a special extension type alongside modules, themes, and profiles. They had their own discovery mechanism through ThemeEngineExtensionList, .engine files, and .info.yml files in the core/themes/engines/ directory. This system has been fully removed.

The conversion removes ThemeEngineExtensionList and InstallerThemeEngineExtensionList classes entirely, along with the extension.list.theme_engine service. The ExtensionPathResolver no longer accepts or resolves theme engine paths. Theme engine discovery has been removed from ExtensionDiscovery.

The ActiveTheme class no longer stores or provides the owner property (which pointed to the theme engine's .engine file path). The ThemeExtensionList no longer depends on ThemeEngineExtensionList and uses autowiring for its dependencies. Theme initialization no longer includes .engine files.

All legacy Twig engine functions (twig_theme(), twig_extension(), twig_render_template()) and the twig.engine file have been removed. The theme registry and theme manager now call theme engine services directly through the tagged service system. The test theme engine and its associated tests have been removed.

Performance test assertions were updated to reflect reduced cache lookups now that theme engines are no longer scanned at runtime.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: February 24, 2026
  • Committed: March 4, 2026 (8 days later)

Key contributors

  • smustgrave (Mobomo)
  • nicxvan (nLightened Development LLC)
  • berdir (MD Systems GmbH)
  • catch (Third and Grove)

Lessons learned

  • When removing an @internal API with extremely narrow real-world usage, explicit deprecation may be skipped if the likelihood of helping anyone is negligible. Weigh the cost of a multi-release deprecation cycle against actual impact to the ecosystem. (via catch, berdir)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3202329: Outbound path processors miss the route name and parameters
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal feature request
  • Version: 11.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: d590556 · +38/-26 prod · +108/-52 test · 18 files
  • Discussion: #3202329 · 12 contributors · 45 comments

Summary

Outbound path processors can now access route names and parameters when generating URLs. Previously, processors received only the route object, which forced some modules to use workarounds like parsing URLs or injecting services that could create circular dependencies. This enhancement enables modules like Domain to optimize path processing without performance penalties or architectural problems.

Impact

Affects module maintainers.

  • New API: Outbound path processors now receive route_name and route_parameters in the $options array

Technical details

UrlGenerator::generateFromRoute() now adds two new keys to the $options array passed to outbound path processors: route_name (the string route name) and route_parameters (the array of parameters). These join the existing route key that provides the route object.

This change addresses a gap where processors needed route information but had no clean way to access it. The route object alone does not expose the route name or the specific parameters used to generate the current URL. Previously, processors had to call Url::fromUserInput() to extract this information, which is expensive, or inject the route provider or router, which creates circular dependency issues.

The new data is added directly in UrlGenerator::generateFromRoute() right before calling processPath(). The information is already available at that point in the generation flow, so passing it to processors is straightforward. The OutboundPathProcessorInterface documentation now documents these two new options alongside the existing route option.

Test coverage verifies that a mock path processor receives both route_name and route_parameters with the expected values.

Upgrade

Custom outbound path processors can now access $options['route_name'] and $options['route_parameters'] in their processOutbound() method. These keys are automatically populated when URLs are generated from routes.

public function processOutbound($path, &$options = [], Request $request = NULL, BubbleableMetadata $bubbleable_metadata = NULL) {
  if (isset($options['route_name']) && $options['route_name'] === 'entity.node.canonical') {
    $node_id = $options['route_parameters']['node'];
    // Process path based on node ID without expensive lookups.
  }
  return $path;
}

No code changes are required. This is a backward-compatible addition of new optional data.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: March 8, 2021
  • First commit: October 29, 2025 (4 years later)
  • Last commit: March 4, 2026 (4 months later)

Key contributors

  • hchonov (1xINTERNET)
  • dieterholvoet (Minsky)
  • riyas_nr (QED42)
  • mably (Bordeaux Metropole)
  • catch (Third and Grove)
  • dcam
  • longwave (Full Fat Things)

Collaboration

This feature request took nearly five years from opening to final commit. The change was committed in October 2025 but had to be reverted due to test annotation issues. dcam fixed the @legacy-covers attribute problem in late 2025, allowing the feature to be recommitted in March 2026. The long timeline reflects the challenge of adding new API data without breaking backward compatibility. Contributors from Domain module development drove the use case, showing how contrib module needs can shape core improvements. The straightforward technical change belied the care needed to get testing right for core inclusion.

Lessons learned

  • When processing paths through a pipeline of processors, pass the original routing context (route name and parameters) alongside the derived data (route object and path). Without the original context, processors must reverse-engineer information from the path, causing performance problems and circular dependencies when trying to access the route provider or router. (via dieterholvoet, mably)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3319212: Entities without labels cause TypeError in EntityController title callbacks
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal bug report
  • Version: 11.3.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 20de794 · +48/-9 prod · +171/-0 test · 6 files
  • Discussion: #3319212 · 11 contributors · 33 comments

Summary

When a custom entity type has no label field, visiting its edit or delete page would crash with a TypeError. This happened because the title callbacks expected entity labels to always be strings, but entities without label fields return NULL. The fix ensures these pages display generic "Edit" or "Delete" titles instead of crashing when an entity has no label.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The EntityController::editTitle() and EntityController::deleteTitle() methods generate page titles by calling $entity->label() and passing the result to translatable strings like "Edit %label" and "Delete %label". When an entity type has no label field, label() returns NULL, which caused a TypeError in Html::escape() because it expects a string parameter.

The fix splits the logic into explicit checks. First, it verifies the entity exists (returning NULL or empty string as before to maintain backward compatibility). Then it checks if label() returns NULL. If so, it returns just "Edit" or "Delete" without the placeholder. If a label exists, it returns the original formatted string with the label included.

A new unit test covers both scenarios: entities with labels (which should display "Edit example" or "Delete example") and entities without labels (which should display just "Edit" or "Delete"). The test uses PHPUnit attributes to parameterize test cases for both callbacks and both label states.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: November 4, 2022
  • Committed: March 4, 2026 (3 years later)

Key contributors

  • joachim (Factorial GmbH)
  • andrew answer
  • dcam
  • longwave (Full Fat Things)

Collaboration

This issue remained open for over three years before being resolved. The initial patch approach used a ternary operator with an empty string fallback, which would have produced trailing spaces in titles. Community reviewers identified this issue and suggested using proper conditionals instead.

The path forward became clear when dcam converted the patch to a merge request, added comprehensive unit tests, and discovered the same bug existed in both editTitle() and deleteTitle() callbacks. This expanded the scope appropriately. Several iterations refined the code structure, with joachim pushing for cleaner conditional logic rather than mixing ternary and if/else patterns. The final version uses straightforward null checks that are easy to understand and maintain.

The issue was committed to Drupal 11.3 and later versions only, as it did not backport cleanly to 10.6.

Lessons learned

  • When displaying entity labels in user-facing strings, provide fallback handling for NULL or empty labels. Entity types may legitimately lack labels, and passing NULL to string formatting functions causes TypeErrors. Use a ternary or conditional to substitute a generic string (like 'Edit') or the entity ID when label() returns NULL. (via andrew answer, joachim, dcam)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3228209: Move hasRole() method from UserInterface to AccountInterface
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal task
  • Version: 11.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 6e3d95b · +57/-75 · 20 files
  • Discussion: #3228209 · 17 contributors · 50 comments

Summary

The hasRole() method has been added to AccountInterface, making it available on all account objects. Previously, this method was only available on UserSession and AccountProxy classes, or by loading the full user entity. This change provides a consistent API for checking user roles across different account implementations, eliminating the need to load the full user entity just to check roles. The method was already implemented in the concrete classes, so this change formalizes the interface contract.

Impact

Affects module maintainers.

  • New API: AccountInterface::hasRole() method added to check if an account has a specific role.

Technical details

In a previous change (#2991232), the hasRole() method was added to AccountProxy and UserSession classes but not to AccountInterface to maintain backward compatibility with existing implementations. This issue completes that work by moving the method definition to AccountInterface.

The change adds hasRole(string $rid) to AccountInterface and removes the duplicate method definition from UserInterface (which extends AccountInterface). The User entity class signature was updated to include the string type hint for the $rid parameter to match the interface. TODO comments referencing this issue were removed from AccountProxy and UserSession.

The implementation uses in_array() to check if the role ID exists in the array returned by getRoles(). This provides a standard way to check roles without needing to load the full user entity when working with account proxy or session objects.

After initially being committed to Drupal 11.4, the change was briefly reverted due to test failures, then re-committed successfully.

Upgrade

If you have custom code that implements AccountInterface, you must now implement the hasRole() method:

public function hasRole(string $rid) {
  return in_array($rid, $this->getRoles(), TRUE);
}

Most implementations can follow the same pattern as UserSession by checking if the role ID exists in the array returned by getRoles().

See the change record at https://www.drupal.org/node/3283218 for more details.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: August 15, 2021
  • First commit: March 3, 2026 (4 years later)
  • Last commit: March 4, 2026 (1 day later)

Key contributors

  • matroskeen (DevBranch)
  • dww (TEN7)
  • alexpott (Acro Commerce)
  • berdir (MD Systems GmbH)
  • catch (Third and Grove)
  • rosk0 (Catalyst IT)
  • pritishkumar (OpenSense Labs)
  • shalini_jha (QED42)
  • longwave (Full Fat Things)

Collaboration

This issue was opened in August 2021 as follow-up work to #2991232, which added hasRole() implementations to concrete classes. The issue waited over three years for the right time to make this interface change. Early discussion explored whether the separation between AccountInterface and UserInterface was still meaningful, and whether adding methods to interfaces violated backward compatibility policies.

The work sat dormant until early 2025 when RoSk0 rebased the code to Drupal 11.x. After debate about return type hints and BC policy compliance, longwave made the decision to commit this for 11.4, noting "it's been waiting long enough and nobody has any better ideas." The change was initially reverted after breaking tests, then successfully re-committed after fixes were applied.

Lessons learned

  • When adding methods to interfaces in a minor release, you can do so without breaking backward compatibility if the interface is not tagged @api and has a 1:1 or similar relationship with implementing classes or base classes/traits. This allows new features to be added while giving module authors the option to implement the method or rely on a base implementation. (via catch)
  • When a framework maintains separate interfaces for runtime session objects (lightweight, no database dependency) and full entity objects (full data access), moving methods between them creates a tight coupling. If code commonly needs to load the full entity from the session object to access a method, the abstraction boundary may be unclear, but consolidating interfaces requires careful consideration of the dependency implications. (via berdir)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3575542: Remove deprecations related to the theme preprocess discovery and theme.inc
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal task
  • Version: main
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 4eb589c · +49/-899 prod · +15/-167 test · +0/-6 infra · 25 files
  • Discussion: #3575542 · 4 contributors · 21 comments

Summary

Drupal 12 removes several theme system features that were deprecated in earlier versions. The theme registry no longer discovers template_preprocess_*() functions automatically, and the file and includes properties in hook_theme() definitions are removed. Theme engines can no longer register preprocess functions. Many deprecated preprocess functions from core/includes/theme.inc are deleted entirely.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

This removal targets multiple deprecation notices introduced between Drupal 11.1 and 11.3. The theme registry (Registry class) no longer auto-discovers procedural template_preprocess_*() functions. Modules must now register preprocess callbacks explicitly using the initial preprocess key in hook_theme() definitions.

The file and includes properties in hook_theme() are removed entirely. These allowed modules to specify include files containing preprocess functions. All preprocess logic must now be in autoloaded classes.

Theme engines previously could register preprocess callbacks using naming patterns like {engine_name}_engine_preprocess_{hook}(). This capability is removed with no replacement.

The Registry and ThemeManager constructors are simplified. The $root parameter is removed from Registry, and the optional $theme_name parameter is eliminated. Tests that needed to work with multiple themes now switch the active theme via the theme manager instead of instantiating separate registry instances.

Many procedural preprocess functions from theme.inc are removed: template_preprocess(), template_preprocess_html(), template_preprocess_page(), template_preprocess_links(), template_preprocess_image(), template_preprocess_table(), and numerous others. These functions now exist only as service methods registered directly in hook_theme() definitions.

Upgrade

If you have custom modules or themes extending Registry or ThemeManager, update constructor signatures. The $root parameter is removed from Registry as the first argument. The optional $theme_name parameter is also removed from Registry.

If you defined template_preprocess_*() functions, register them explicitly in your hook_theme() implementation using the initial preprocess key:

function mymodule_theme(): array {
  return [
    'my_template' => [
      'variables' => ['content' => NULL],
      'initial preprocess' => [MyClass::class, 'preprocessMyTemplate'],
    ],
  ];
}

Remove any file or includes keys from your hook_theme() definitions. Move preprocess functions into autoloaded classes.

If you maintained a theme engine with preprocess functions, those functions will no longer be invoked. Migrate this logic into theme preprocess hooks or contributed module code.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: February 24, 2026
  • Committed: March 3, 2026 (6 days later)

Key contributors

  • berdir (MD Systems GmbH)
  • nicxvan (nLightened Development LLC)
  • dcam
  • catch (Third and Grove)

Collaboration

This issue was opened by berdir on February 24 and committed March 3, 2026, spanning just over one week. The original scope included removing deprecations for preprocess discovery and the theme.inc file. Berdir expanded the scope during development, removing the $theme_name constructor parameter from Registry and eliminating test complexity around theme switching.

Nicxvan provided thorough review feedback on constructor improvements, noting property promotion opportunities and leftover test comments. Dcam identified lingering references to removed functions throughout core documentation. Both reviewers verified that only deprecated code was removed and that all references were cleaned up. Berdir addressed all feedback promptly, implementing property promotion and updating documentation references. The collaboration demonstrated efficient coordination with minimal iteration, moving from initial patch to RTBC in just a few review rounds.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3556948: Merging Gin as Admin theme
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal task
  • Version: main
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 81ac7ad · +23942/-0 prod · +8/-12 test · +2/-0 infra · 200 files
  • Discussion: #3556948 · 19 contributors · 66 comments

Summary

A new experimental admin theme called Admin has been added to Drupal core. This theme evolved from the contributed Gin theme and provides a modern editing experience with improved usability. It includes two feature flags: one to control sticky action buttons on forms and another to optionally use the admin theme for the login page. The theme is experimental in its first release, meaning it will continue evolving before becoming stable. Site owners can enable it to test the new interface but should expect ongoing changes.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The Admin theme is a fork of the Gin contrib theme being merged into core under a new machine name. The merge includes approximately 10,000 lines of CSS, JavaScript, templates, and PHP across base styles, components, and layout files. The theme overrides and extends many core libraries including dropbuttons, vertical tabs, dialogs, tabledrag, and form elements.

Two settings are available: $settings['core_admin_theme_use_sticky_action_buttons'] controls where sticky action buttons appear (values: never, content_forms, always), and $settings['user_login_admin_theme'] determines if the login page uses the admin theme (defaults to TRUE).

The theme includes hooks for contrib modules to register routes that should use the content form layout (hook_admin_content_form_routes() and its alter hook). Much of the code still references "gin" internally in CSS classes, JavaScript variables, and settings keys. A deduplication effort with Claro components remains in a migration directory and will be completed in follow-up issues. The theme extends rather than replaces Claro initially, maintaining three admin themes during the transition period.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: November 10, 2025
  • First commit: March 1, 2026 (3 months later)
  • Last commit: March 3, 2026 (1 day later)

Key contributors

  • jurgenhaas (LakeDrops)
  • quietone (PreviousNext)
  • pameeela (Technocrat)
  • longwave (Full Fat Things)
  • saschaeggi
  • berdir (MD Systems GmbH)
  • catch (Third and Grove)
  • lauriii (Acquia)
  • phenaproxima (Acquia)

Collaboration

This issue moved from opened to committed in about four months, but the underlying work spanned much longer through the Gin contrib project. The merge required coordination across multiple concerns: linting and code standards, test compatibility, accessibility considerations, and the question of whether certain features like login theme negotiation should be included initially.

The size of the merge request (nearly 50 changed files visible in the diff summary) made review challenging. Several reviewers noted the difficulty of meaningful review for such a large addition, though the decision to merge as experimental was made to enable iterative improvement. @phenaproxima fixed a mysterious test failure caused by a line dating back to 2012. @longwave addressed PHPStan and linting issues. @saschaeggi and @jurgenhaas drove the primary implementation work.

Discussion touched on whether the machine name "admin" would cause confusion, leading to a follow-up issue. Accessibility issues present in Gin were acknowledged, with the consensus that merging into core would increase attention and fixes. The commit happened early in the Drupal 11 release cycle as experimental, allowing the theme to evolve before stabilization.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3576431: Mark new admin theme as experimental
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Critical task
  • Version: main
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 91b003d · +2/-2 · 2 files
  • Discussion: #3576431 · 5 contributors · 14 comments

Summary

The Admin theme in Drupal core was incorrectly marked as experimental using an outdated metadata format. The theme used experimental: true in its configuration file, but the correct format is lifecycle: experimental. This change updates the Admin theme to use the proper lifecycle metadata syntax. The visual result on the Appearance page remains the same, showing the experimental status correctly. This is a metadata correction that ensures the theme follows Drupal's current standards for marking experimental features.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The Admin theme's admin.info.yml file contained experimental: true, which is an obsolete way to mark a theme as experimental. Drupal's current standard uses the lifecycle key with a value of experimental instead.

This change updates the single line in admin.info.yml from experimental: true to lifecycle: experimental. Both formats display the experimental status on the Appearance page, but the lifecycle format is the current standard used across Drupal core.

The lifecycle metadata system is used to communicate the maturity level of themes and modules. Using the correct format ensures consistency with other experimental components in core and makes the status machine-readable in the expected format.

This is a metadata-only change with no functional impact. The theme continues to work exactly the same way, and the experimental warning continues to appear on the Appearance page. The change simply corrects the metadata format to match current Drupal standards.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: March 1, 2026
  • Committed: March 2, 2026

Key contributors

  • nicxvan (nLightened Development LLC)
  • kentr
  • rkoller

Collaboration

This issue was reported and fixed quickly, moving from opened to first commit in one day. The reporter identified the incorrect metadata format and provided a straightforward fix. Screenshots were added to document that the visual result on the Appearance page remains consistent before and after the change.

During review, discussion touched on broader usability concerns about the Appearance page layout, linking to #2860419 and #3249374, though these were outside the scope of this metadata fix.

The maintainer noted uncertainty about whether the Admin theme would land in Drupal 11.x, awaiting clarification on the backport plan.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3576074: Current user is changed unexpectedly
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Major bug report
  • Version: main
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: d14a214 · +114/-3 prod · +318/-0 test · 9 files
  • Discussion: #3576074 · 3 contributors · 24 comments

Summary

Drupal 11.3 introduced a bug where permission checks on one user account could unexpectedly change the current user identity in unrelated parts of the page. This happened when render arrays used lazy builders and one checked permissions for a different user than the currently logged-in user. The fix ensures that temporary user switches during permission calculation stay confined to their intended scope and do not leak to other parts of the rendering process.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The bug occurred in AccessPolicyProcessor::processAccessPolicies(), which temporarily switches the current user when calculating permissions for a different account. This is necessary for correct variation cache behavior. The problem arose when fibers—PHP's cooperative multitasking feature used in lazy builder rendering—suspended execution during this user switch. If a fiber suspended while entities were loading (such as roles), execution could return to a different fiber before the user was switched back, causing the wrong user identity to persist.

The fix wraps the permission calculation logic in a child fiber when already running inside a fiber. This child fiber is immediately and repeatedly resumed if it suspends, preventing execution from escaping to the parent fiber until the user switch is complete. The actual permission logic moved to a new protected method doProcessAccessPolicies(). When not running in a fiber, the method is called directly without overhead.

A comprehensive kernel test reproduces the issue by creating two lazy builders: one that displays the current user ID and another that checks permissions on a different user. Without the fix, the test fails because the first lazy builder incorrectly shows the second user's ID.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: February 26, 2026
  • Committed: March 2, 2026 (3 days later)

Key contributors

  • claudiu.cristea (Webikon)
  • godotislate
  • catch (Third and Grove)

Collaboration

The issue was reported by claudiu.cristea after encountering the bug in a production environment using a contrib patch that creates dummy users for access checks. The root cause was quickly identified with help from catch, who confirmed this was a core bug related to fiber suspension during user switching. godotislate created a failing test in MR 14937 that clearly demonstrated the issue and initially explored a more complex solution involving a fiber stack to track user changes per fiber. After determining that approach was too complex and not appropriate for a patch release, godotislate implemented the current fix using a child fiber to contain the user switch. The fix was tested and confirmed working on the original production site. The change was committed to 11.3.x, 11.x, and main four days after the issue was opened. A follow-up issue #3576594 was created to explore avoiding the user switch entirely.

Lessons learned

  • When calculating permissions or access for a user other than the current user, switching the global current user can cause unexpected behavior if asynchronous execution (such as fiber suspension) allows control to leave the function before the user is switched back. Contain the account switch within a child fiber and resume that fiber any time it suspends, ensuring the execution does not leave until the current user has been restored. (via godotislate, catch)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3572628: Mobile sidebar should close by ESCAPE key
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Major bug report
  • Version: 11.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 302f907 · +66/-6 prod · +80/-10 test · 10 files
  • Discussion: #3572628 · 6 contributors · 35 comments

Summary

The mobile Navigation sidebar can now be closed by pressing the Escape key, meeting accessibility requirements. Previously, keyboard users had no way to close the mobile sidebar with the Escape key, creating an inequitable experience compared to mouse users. The fix implements a hierarchical behavior: Escape closes submenus first, and if no submenu is open, it closes the entire sidebar and returns focus to the "Expand sidebar" button. This completes a critical accessibility gap identified in the Navigation module's accessibility review.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

This change adds two key behaviors to the Navigation module's mobile sidebar. First, in admin-toolbar-wrapper.js, a new keydown event listener detects the Escape key when the sidebar is expanded and triggers the mobile toggle button to close it. When the sidebar closes via user input, focus moves to the "Expand sidebar" button, following WCAG best practices for focus management in slide-out navigation.

Second, the arrow-navigation.js file now implements hierarchical Escape key handling. The code checks if the user is inside a popover submenu before closing. If they are inside a popover that is open or on a non-trigger element, Escape closes only that submenu using stopPropagation() to prevent the event from bubbling to the parent handler. If no submenu is open, the Escape key bubbles to the document-level handler and closes the entire sidebar.

The template adds data-drupal-selector="admin-toolbar-mobile-trigger" to identify the expand button for focus management. New Nightwatch tests verify both wide and narrow viewport behaviors, including focus return to the trigger button. This incorporates code from issue #3541741 that fixed submenu Escape handling.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: February 10, 2026
  • Committed: March 2, 2026 (20 days later)

Key contributors

  • kentr
  • smustgrave (Mobomo)
  • rkoller
  • mherchel (Dripyard)
  • catch (Third and Grove)
  • lauriii (Acquia)

Collaboration

This issue was the last high-priority accessibility issue blocking the Navigation module's accessibility review in #3391723. It was originally postponed on #3443571 to avoid merge conflicts, then unblocked when that focus trap issue landed. The team discussed working on it at Florida DrupalCamp 2026's contribution sprint, but kentr completed the implementation beforehand. The solution incorporated code from #3541741 by hinal05, which fixed a related submenu-closing issue. After manual testing by lauriii confirmed the behavior, catch committed it to both main and 11.x branches given the strong consensus on approach and its role as the final blocker.

Lessons learned

  • When implementing keyboard navigation for layered UI elements (e.g., sidebar with sub-menus), the ESC key should close the most recently opened layer. If already at the top level, ESC should close the entire component and return focus to the trigger element that opened it, creating an equitable experience between keyboard and pointer users. (via kentr, mherchel)

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3534144: [12.x] [meta] Set Drupal 12 platform and browser requirements by Jan 15 2026
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Critical plan
  • Version: main
  • Status: Fixed
  • Discussion: #3534144 · 5 contributors · 33 comments

Summary

Drupal 12 platform requirements have been established to ensure sites run on secure, well-supported infrastructure. The most significant change is requiring PHP 8.5, following the new policy to adopt the latest stable PHP version at beta release. Database requirements reflect conservative increases that balance access to modern features with practical hosting constraints. Most database versions remain the same or receive modest bumps, recognizing that database upgrades are harder for hosting providers than PHP updates. These requirements align with Ubuntu LTS releases and vendor support cycles to maximize compatibility.

Impact

Affects site owners.

  • Requirements: Drupal 12 will require PHP 8.5, MySQL 8.0 or higher, MariaDB 10.11 or higher, PostgreSQL 18 or higher, and SQLite 3.45 or higher.

Technical details

This meta issue coordinated the platform requirement decisions for Drupal 12 across multiple subsystems. The PHP 8.5 requirement implements the recently approved policy to require the highest stable PHP release available when a major version reaches beta.

For databases, the team applied draft policies that consider vendor LTS cycles, Ubuntu availability, and end-of-life dates. MySQL stays at 8.0 despite being near EOL because hosts struggle with database upgrades and the security surface is low. MariaDB increases from 10.6 to 10.11 to follow their LTS policy more closely. PostgreSQL bumps from 16 to 18, adopting the version available six months before release. SQLite remains at 3.45 since no pressing features require a newer version.

The Composer requirement recommends version 2.9.3 for security but doesn't enforce it strictly since Package Manager now uses local Composer when available. The decisions prioritize making Drupal 12 adoption easier for hosts while ensuring sites run on secure platforms. Multiple documentation pages were updated following the announcement.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: July 4, 2025

Key contributors

  • quietone (PreviousNext)
  • catch (Third and Grove)
  • xjm (Zoocha)
  • gábor hojtsy (Acquia)
  • longwave (Full Fat Things)

Collaboration

This meta issue brought together core committers and release managers to establish platform requirements through collaborative discussion. quietone coordinated the issue, drafted the announcement, and systematically updated all documentation pages. catch clarified policy implementation and provided analysis on database security surfaces versus PHP. longwave researched Ubuntu LTS shipping versions and proposed starting points for each database. xjm offered historical perspective on minimum versus recommended versions. gábor hojtsy updated documentation and published the official announcement at https://www.drupal.org/about/core/blog/announcing-drupal-1200-platform-requirements. The team balanced adoption ease with security and modern feature access.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.

item.title: #3570235: Deprecate functions in syslog.module
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal task
  • Version: 11.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Diff: 4dcc789 · +80/-16 · 4 files
  • Discussion: #3570235 · 4 contributors · 24 comments

Summary

The Syslog module's two remaining procedural functions are now deprecated because they were only used internally by the module itself. The module has been refactored to use modern dependency injection and config targets, making the configuration form simpler and more maintainable. This cleanup removes unnecessary public API surface while keeping all functionality working exactly as before. No contributed modules were found to be using these functions, so the deprecation has no known external impact.

Impact

Affects module maintainers.

  • Deprecation: syslog_facility_list() and syslog_logging_settings_submit() deprecated without replacement; functionality moved to protected methods in SyslogHooks class

Technical details

Two functions in syslog.module have been deprecated: syslog_facility_list() and syslog_logging_settings_submit(). Both were only used internally by the Syslog module to build and process its configuration form.

The functionality has been moved into the SyslogHooks class. The facility list is now a protected facilityList() method called directly from the form alter hook. The submit handler is no longer needed because the form now uses config targets (#config_target) to automatically save values to syslog.settings configuration.

Additional improvements include proper dependency injection of ModuleHandlerInterface into the hooks class, and removal of manual configuration loading and default value setting that config targets handle automatically. The form now relies on the Form API's built-in config target system to handle both populating default values and saving submitted values.

These functions are deprecated without replacement since they were never intended as public API and no known external usage exists.

Upgrade

If you are calling syslog_facility_list() or syslog_logging_settings_submit(), you must update your code. These functions are deprecated in Drupal 11.4.0 and will be removed in Drupal 12.0.0 and 13.0.0 respectively.

There is no direct replacement. If you need a list of syslog facilities, implement your own array:

$facilities = [
  LOG_LOCAL0 => 'LOG_LOCAL0',
  LOG_LOCAL1 => 'LOG_LOCAL1',
  // ... etc
];

If you were relying on the submit handler, use config targets in your form instead:

$form['my_field'] = [
  '#type' => 'textfield',
  '#config_target' => 'my_module.settings:my_key',
];

See #3566774 for details.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: January 29, 2026
  • Committed: March 1, 2026 (1 month later)

Key contributors

  • nicxvan (nLightened Development LLC)
  • mstrelan
  • dcam
  • longwave (Full Fat Things)

Collaboration

nicxvan opened the issue and drove the refactoring to completion. dcam provided thorough review across multiple iterations, including verifying that no contributed modules actually used these functions despite initial search results suggesting otherwise, and conducting manual testing of the form functionality after each change. longwave suggested further simplification by removing the default value keys since config targets handle that automatically, reducing code even more.

This summary is AI-generated and may contain errors. Check the original issue on Drupal.org for the full picture. For more digests, see Drupal Digests.
